stimulus:
Body Length and Defensive Compound Production in Fire Salamanders after Three Weeks at Different Larval Densities

Larval densityAverage body length (mm)Average number of distinct alkaloid compounds per larvaAverage alkaloid amount per larva (ng)Average alkaloid concentration (ng per mm of body length)
High14.211.4425.329.9
Medium17.611.0378.921.5
Low18.011.2342.119.0

Ecologist Helena Bauer and colleagues studied within-species competition among fire salamanders, a species that produces a suite of defensive alkaloid compounds in its skin. The researchers raised fire-salamander larvae for three weeks in tanks of varying larval density but fixed food supply. Based on the post-three-week measurements, the researchers concluded that increased competition shifted resources toward defensive-compound production at the expense of body growth.
stem: Which choice uses data from the table to most effectively support the researchers’ conclusion?

answerOptions.content: The difference in average body length between the low and medium density conditions was small, and between the low and high conditions substantially larger.
The average number of distinct alkaloid compounds per larva remained nearly constant across all three density conditions.
Larvae in the high density condition had a relatively modest increase in alkaloid amount but roughly 1.5 times the concentration found in the low density condition.
Larvae in the low and medium density conditions had substantially lower average alkaloid concentrations but greater average body lengths than those in the high density condition.

rationale: Choice D is the best answer because the researchers’ conclusion has two linked components: more competition increases defensive compounds, and growth suffers. Choice D captures both—the high-density group had a higher alkaloid concentration AND smaller body length than the lower-density groups—making the trade-off explicit. Choice A speaks only to growth. Choice B doesn’t support either claim. Choice C captures the toxin-side increase but not the growth cost.
